searchDocs.title: For​
searchDocs.content: </> Delay:For( Value: any, Time: number ) → () Sets a custom wait time for a value

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Animation.Destroy() → () Cleans up the animation object and removes it from any associated threads.

searchDocs.title: Stop​
searchDocs.content: </> Animation.Stop() → () Stops the animation if it is currently playing.

searchDocs.title: Skip​
searchDocs.content: </> Animation.Skip() → () Immediately applies the animation's goal state to the target entity.

searchDocs.title: Do And State
searchDocs.content: Do And State

searchDocs.title: Special Faye Values​
searchDocs.content: Faye also offers some utilities that let you sync Values to properties and Attributes  local Value = Thread:InstanceAttributeSync(Instance,"SomeAttribute") -- Attribute sync local Value = Thread:InstancePropertySync(Instance,"Size") -- Property sync   You can use these special values like you would regular values, it has support for almost all functions if not all.

searchDocs.title: Basics​
searchDocs.content: Placing events in the props tables, will connect the listener  { Touched = function() end }   The first parameter of the events is always the Instance being worked on and following is all the other parameters  { Touched = function(Entity, PartTouched) end }   note Yes i am using an event reserved for parts, you can create anything with Faye.  Utilities help you implement special properties, reactivity, and animations to your Faye Instances. Utilities also work on Instances that are Configured using the Configure function.

searchDocs.title: MethodSignaled Utility​
searchDocs.content: The MethodSignaled utility allows you to listen to complex instance events like, GetAttributeChangedSignal, GetPropertyChangedSignal, etc...  { [Thread:MethodSignaled("GetPropertyChangedSignal","Size")] = function(Entity) local Size = Entity.AbsoluteSize print(Size) end, }   Its important to remember that complex roblox instance events don't return any parameters, so the only one that will be there is the one Faye automatically adds there which is the Entity in the first parameter position.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:Destroy() → () Destroys the delay value object and cleans up its resources.

searchDocs.title: Call​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Call() → () Calls all tasks in the Space.

searchDocs.title: Add​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Add(Params: {[any]: any}) → () Adds/Creates a task to the Space. The task will be called when the Space is called.

searchDocs.title: Remove​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Remove(Task: Task) → () Removes a task from the Space. The task will no longer be called when the Space is called.

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Connect( Connection: RBXScriptSignal , Id: string? ) → () Connects a signal to space, pass an id if u only want that connection to fire a specific task.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Destroy() → () Cleans up the Space and removes all tasks. This will also disconnect any connections made to the Space.

searchDocs.title: SetId​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:SetId(Id: string) → () Sets a unique ID for the task. This ID can be used to identify the task later.

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:Connect() → () Connects a signal to the task. The task will be called when the signal is fired.

searchDocs.title: AddParameter​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:AddParameter() → () Cleans up the task and removes it from the Space. This will also disconnect any connections made to the task.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:Destroy() → () Cleans up the task and removes it from the Space. This will also disconnect any connections made to the task.

searchDocs.title: Call​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:Call() → () Executes the task.

searchDocs.title: Props​
searchDocs.content: When Creating or Configuring an item, the table is called a 'Props Table'  Thread:Create "Frame" { --this is a props table }   You can set properties of that entity in the Props Table and you can also use Faye Utilities to achieve reactivity or other stuff.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1,0,0) }

searchDocs.title: Functions​
searchDocs.content: Functions in props tables work like code blocks, everything they return will also be compiled, you can use them to do checks or add conditional components or properties. You can return components, Props tables, etc... if it doesn't error it will probably work.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; function() if true then return Thread:Create "Frame" { ... } else return {BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1,0,0)} end end }   You can also set properties to functions  { BackgroundColor3 = function() if true then return Color3.new(1,0,0) else return Color3.new(0,1,0) end end }   You can nest Props tables as much as you want and u can have as many functions as you want into them.

searchDocs.title: Reactive Animations​
searchDocs.content: Before creating reactive animations, you need to know about the Info tables and the Modification tables  Info​  local Info = require(Faye.Info) local SpringInfo = require(Faye.SpringInfo) local AnimInfo = Info( -- or Thread.Info or Faye.Info Time: number?, EasingStyle: Enum.EasingStyle?, EasingDirection: Enum.EasingDirection?, RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? ) local AnimSpringInfo = SpringInfo( -- or Thread.SpringInfo or Faye.Info Time: number?, Frequency: number?, -- frequency Damping: number?, --loss of energy RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? )   These are similar to to TweenService's TweenInfo, with an Addition of SpringInfo, If you want to Use Springs instead of the plain roblox easings you substitute the Info with a SpringInfo when creating an animation, its that easy.  note If you leave any empty fields, it will automatically fill it with default values. You can also use a table for the info, these 'Info' and 'SpringInfo' functions just help you structure it with suggestions  Modification Tables​  local Modifications = { FirstGoal: any; -- The goal for the first play FirstDelayTime: number?; -- the DelayTime for the first play FirstInfo: (Info | SpringInfo)?; -- the Info for the first play From: any; -- makes it so the animation starts from this once AlwaysFrom: any; --makes it so the animation always start from this }   These are self explanatory and allow you to have special modifications for your animations  Animation Creation​  The first parameter is the goal, the second the Info, and the third the modification table, the only mandatory parameter is the goal, the rest can be left empty.  Thread:Create "Frame" { BackgroundColor3 = Thread:Animation(Color3.new(1,1,0),InfoTable); -- tweens smoothly to the color }   Above we have a tween that starts from the current Frame Color to the Goal  --test this code yourself in studio local Faye = require(...) -- set this to the right path return function(Target) local Thread = Faye.new() Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; BackgroundColor3 = Thread:Animation( Color3.new(1,1,0) , Thread.Info(.5) , {From = Color3.new(1,0,0)} ); -- Starts from red } return function() Thread:Destroy() end end     Rather than our goal, we can substitute it with a Faye Value, and everytime we update it, our Frame will react to its change  local Goal = Thread:Value(Color3.new(1,1,0)) Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; BackgroundColor3 = Thread:Animation( Goal , Thread.Info(.5) , {From = Color3.new(1,0,0)} ); -- Starts from red MouseEnter = function() Goal:Set(Color3.new(0,0,1)) end, MouseLeave = function() Goal:Reset() end }     note When using Faye values as Goal, the animation won't tween to the goal for the first iteration, it will always set the Instance's property to the Goal's initial value first, so if you want it to tween it to, you must specify the From in the Animation modifications table, then it will tween to the Goal rather than setting the object's property on initialization.

searchDocs.title: Quick animations​
searchDocs.content: If you just want to play animations and you don't want to setup reactivity, you can use the Faye's LoadAnimation utility, use it within a thread for the Thread to manage cleaning, but its not necessary, You can use it in any script in ur game.  Thread:LoadAnimation( Entity , {Size = UDim2.fromOffset(200,200)} , Thread.Info(.2) ):Play()   You can tween multiple properties in one object at the same time just like regular roblox tweening, the Info parameter is optional, but the Object and Goal table must be specified.  info Like mentioned in the landing page, i did not cover all the functions here, for that go into the Api section

searchDocs.title: Object Creation and Configuration​
searchDocs.content: You can create objects using the Create function  Thread:Create("Frame") { ... }   The first element created using Thread:Create must have a Parent property inside its props table, its children will automatically have it as a parent in the roblox tree.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; Thread:Create "Frame" { ... } }   The Configure function works on already created Faye Instances, or roblox Instances, it implements them into the Faye ecosystem letting you use Faye Utilities and other stuff on it.  Thread:Configure(PlayerGui.ScreenGui) { Thread:Create "Frame" { ... } }   local Frame = Thread:Create("Frame") { ... } Thread:Configure(Frame) { Position = ... }

searchDocs.title: Project Setup​
searchDocs.content: The ideal component based project setup looks like  local Faye = (...) return function(Parent: Instance) local Thread = Faye.new() ... return function() Thread:Destroy(); end) end)   The Thread allows you to keep track of what you create and allow you to clean everthing with one function. It is not needed, you can use Faye without a Thread as seen below  local Create = require(Faye.Create) return function(Parent: Instance) Create(...) end)   You can reuse some some Utilities, but a minority of them can't be reused, like the Create function, which creates a new Faye Instance, Faye Instances are unique due to the reactivity, so they can't be cloned and this is where the component system you might be familiar with comes in.  local EmptyFrame = function() return Create(...) end local Frame = EmptyFrame()   info If you will use Faye utilities without the Faye Thread, you can also get them with Faye[Utility name], you don't have to require the individual modules

searchDocs.title: CloneAnimation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.CloneAnimation( Animaiton: {any:any}, Modifications: AnimationModifications ) → {any:any}? Clones an animation table and applies any provided modifications.

searchDocs.title: CloneTable​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.CloneTable( Table: {any:any}, cloneFunction: ( Index: any, Value: any ) → ( any?, any? )? ) → {any:any}? Clones a table. If a cloneFunction is provided, it will be used to determine how to clone each entry.

searchDocs.title: SpringInfo​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.SpringInfo( Time: number?, Frequency: number?, Damping: number?, RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? ) → SpringInfo Creates a SpringInfo object that can be used in animations to define spring-like behavior.

searchDocs.title: AdvancedIterate​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:AdvancedIterate( Tab: any, Func: ( Index: any, Value: any, InnerThread: Types.Thread?, Entity: Instance? ) → () ) → () Creates a reactive iterator that reacts to the table's individually changes and updates them accordingly.

searchDocs.title: Animation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Animation( Goal: any,-- The target value or state for the animation Info: Types.Info | Types.SpringInfo?,-- (Optional) The animation info Modifications: Types.AnimationModifications?-- (Optional) Modifications to apply to the animation ) → Animation Creates an animation object with the specified goal, info, and optional modifications.

searchDocs.title: Configure​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Configure(Entity: any) → function Allows configuration on an existing physical or none physical component. Inherits method from Create.

searchDocs.title: Create​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Create(ClassName: string) → (props: PropertyTypes.Property) → Create Creates a new instance object.

searchDocs.title: Do​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Do(func: ( Grab: any, InnerThread: Thread, Entity: Instance? ) → ) → Do Creates a Do object that can be used in Faye's threading system to execute functions.

searchDocs.title: Extend​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Extend(NoLink: boolean) → Thread Creates a sub-thread. Leave the NoLink to nil and the thread will be linked to the parent thread(will delete with parent), set NoLink to true for the thread to be independent.

searchDocs.title: Spawn​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Spawn() → () Similar to Task.spawn, not to be used within a compileable table.

searchDocs.title: Delay​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Delay() → () Similar to Task.Delay, not to be used within a compileable table.

searchDocs.title: Debris​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Debris() → () Similar to game.debris:AddItem, not to be used within a compileable table.

searchDocs.title: Clean​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Clean() → () This method cleans everything added to the thread, and the thread itself. After this method gets called the thread is no longer useable.

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Connect( Connection: RBXScriptSignal , Function:: () → () ) → () Signal connection

searchDocs.title: Add​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Add(Entity: any) → () The entity to add to the thread

searchDocs.title: Remove​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Remove(Entity: any) → () The entity to remove from the thread, meaning when the thread is cleaned it won't be cleaned with it.

searchDocs.title: Schedule​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Schedule( Time: number?, Item: any, ...: any ) → item Cleans an item individually based off the time. can also be used to call functions after a time has passed.

searchDocs.title: InstanceAttributeSync​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:InstanceAttributeSync( Entity: Instance , Property: string ) → InstanceAttributeSync Creates a Value object that syncs with a specific attribute of an Instance.

searchDocs.title: InstancePropertySync​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:InstancePropertySync( Entity: Instance , Property: string ) → InstancePropertySync Creates a Value object that syncs with a specific property of an Instance.

searchDocs.title: Iterate​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Iterate( Tab: any, Func: ( Index: any, Value: any, InnerThread: Thread?, Entity: Instance? ) → () ) → Iterate Creates a reactive iterator, when the value changes it refreshes the entire thing.

searchDocs.title: Listener​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Listener( Entity: any, Event(s): any, Function: function ) → () Connects the given function to the specified event(s) on the entity.

searchDocs.title: LoadAnimation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:LoadAnimation( Entity: Instance ,-- The instance to animate Goal: Types.Properties,-- The goal properties to animate to Info: Types.Info?-- (Optional) The animation info ) → () return PlayableAnimation -- The playable animation object

searchDocs.title: MethodSignaled​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:MethodSignaled( Method: string, Index: string, RunOnInitialization: boolean? ) → () Listener for methods, example: 'GetPropertyChangedSignal', 'GetAttributeChangedSignal', etc.

searchDocs.title: ModifyAnimation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:ModifyAnimation( Animaiton: {any: any}, Modifications: Types.AnimationModifications ) → ModifyAnimation Lets you add modifications to an existing animation object, doesn't work with animation objects created using LoadAnimation.

searchDocs.title: Reactive​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Reactive(func: (Grab: (Value: Instance | Types.Value) → any) → ()) → Reactive Creates a reactive function that re-runs whenever any of the grabbed values change.

searchDocs.title: SetTo​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:SetTo(Value: any) → SetTo Wraps a value into a SetTo value object that can be used in props tables.

searchDocs.title: Signal​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Signal( Connections: RBXScriptSignal | {RBXScriptSignal },-- The signal or list of signals to connect to func: (...any) → ()?-- The function to execute when the signal is ) → Signal Creates a signal object that listens to the specified connections and executes the provided function in a reactive manner.

searchDocs.title: SignalState​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:SignalState( Connections: RBXScriptSignal | {RBXScriptSignal }, func: (...any) → ()? ) → SignalState Creates a reactive signal that runs the provided function whenever any of the provided connections fire, this one deletes the old components before loading the new returned returned ones.

searchDocs.title: Space​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Space(func: function) → () Creates a new Space object. The space will call the function when the space is called.

searchDocs.title: SpecialThread​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:SpecialThread( func: ( Thread: Types.Thread, Entity: Instance ) → (), Params: (Types.SpecialThreadParams | number)? ) → SpecialThread Creates a special thread object that can be used in Faye's threading system with custom settings.

searchDocs.title: State​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:State(func: ( Grab: any, InnerThread: Thread, Entity: Instance? ) → ()) → State Creates a State object that can be used in Faye's reactive system, it deletes the old components before loading the new ones.

searchDocs.title: Value​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Value(Initial: any) → Value Creates a Value object that can be used in Faye's reactive system.

searchDocs.title: YieldSafe​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:YieldSafe( func: () → (), DelayTime: number? ) → YieldSafe Creates a YieldSafe object that can be used to run functions with yields safely.
